skill.name: azure-ai-voicelive-py
skill.description: Guidance for building Python applications with Azure AI Voice Live, including WebSocket sessions, audio streaming, turn detection, function calling, and transcription.
skill.summary: Build real-time Python voice applications with Azure AI Voice Live.
skill.author: sickn33

security_audit.summary: Static analysis reported critical heuristic combinations, many shell execution hits, base64 usage, hidden file access, and weak crypto. Review found no prompt injection or malicious intent in SKILL.md; most severe static findings are false positives from Markdown formatting, URL scopes, and audio base64 handling. The remaining risk is medium because the skill teaches credentialed network connections and real-time audio streaming to Azure services.

medium_findings.title: Credentialed Azure Voice Live Network Examples
medium_findings.description: The skill shows code that reads Azure endpoint and API key values from environment variables and opens Azure Cognitive Services connections. This is expected for the SDK, but users must protect keys and understand that audio and transcripts may be sent to Azure services.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The environment variable reads and Azure endpoint scope are explicit. The behavior is legitimate SDK usage, but it combines credentials with outbound network access.

medium_findings.title: Real-Time Audio Stream Handling
medium_findings.description: The examples encode microphone audio and decode model audio deltas for live playback. This is core functionality, but it has privacy impact because user speech may be transmitted to a remote model service.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The audio encode and decode examples are directly shown. The privacy concern follows from the documented live audio streaming workflow.

medium_findings.title: Function Call Handler Requires Application Validation
medium_findings.description: The event handling example passes model-supplied function call arguments to an application handler and returns the result to the conversation. The skill does not show validation, allowlisting, or authorization checks for function calls.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The handler is an illustrative placeholder, so exploitability depends on the application implementation. The pattern still needs validation guidance for production use.

low_findings.title: Markdown Code Fences Flagged As Shell Execution
low_findings.description: Most external command findings are false positives caused by Markdown code fences and inline backticks around examples. The only actual shell command shown is package installation, which is normal setup guidance for this SDK skill.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited lines are documentation formatting or a standard pip install command. No dynamic command construction or execution by the skill is present.

low_findings.title: Base64 Audio Processing Is Not Obfuscation
low_findings.description: The static analyzer flagged base64 decoding, but the surrounding text identifies it as PCM audio transport for Azure Voice Live events. This is a normal audio streaming format, not evidence of hidden payloads.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The code is explicitly within audio send and receive sections. The values are SDK event audio deltas, not decoded executable content.

low_findings.title: Weak Cryptography Findings Are False Positives
low_findings.description: The weak cryptographic algorithm findings do not correspond to cryptographic operations in the cited lines. The cited locations describe the skill, a weather function example, and a voice option table.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited lines do not contain hash algorithms, cipher configuration, or cryptographic API calls. The labels appear to be keyword collisions.

low_findings.title: Hidden File Access Findings Are URL Scope False Positives
low_findings.description: The filesystem findings point to the Azure credential scope ending in .default. This is a URL scope string, not a hidden file path or filesystem access.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Both locations are credential scope strings for Azure Cognitive Services. There is no file read or write operation at those lines.

dangerous_patterns.title: Credentialed Azure Voice Live Network Examples
dangerous_patterns.description: The skill shows code that reads Azure endpoint and API key values from environment variables and opens Azure Cognitive Services connections. This is expected for the SDK, but users must protect keys and understand that audio and transcripts may be sent to Azure services.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The environment variable reads and Azure endpoint scope are explicit. The behavior is legitimate SDK usage, but it combines credentials with outbound network access.

dangerous_patterns.title: Real-Time Audio Stream Handling
dangerous_patterns.description: The examples encode microphone audio and decode model audio deltas for live playback. This is core functionality, but it has privacy impact because user speech may be transmitted to a remote model service.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The audio encode and decode examples are directly shown. The privacy concern follows from the documented live audio streaming workflow.

dangerous_patterns.title: Function Call Handler Requires Application Validation
dangerous_patterns.description: The event handling example passes model-supplied function call arguments to an application handler and returns the result to the conversation. The skill does not show validation, allowlisting, or authorization checks for function calls.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The handler is an illustrative placeholder, so exploitability depends on the application implementation. The pattern still needs validation guidance for production use.

content.value_statement: Real-time voice applications require reliable audio streaming, session setup, and cloud authentication. This skill gives Python patterns for Azure AI Voice Live conversations, transcription, VAD, and function calling.
actual_capabilities: Shows how to install and import the Azure AI Voice Live Python SDK.
Demonstrates DefaultAzureCredential and API key authentication patterns.
Explains session configuration for voices, modalities, audio formats, and server VAD.
Provides examples for sending and receiving base64 PCM16 audio streams.
Covers event handling for transcripts, responses, errors, and function calls.
Lists voice options, audio formats, and turn detection modes.
limitations: It is documentation guidance, not a complete runnable application.
It assumes access to Azure AI Voice Live and valid Azure credentials.
It does not implement microphone capture, playback, or production deployment code.
It does not provide detailed security controls for function calling workflows.

use_cases.title: Prototype a Voice Assistant
use_cases.description: Create a Python proof of concept that streams speech to Azure AI and receives spoken responses.

use_cases.title: Add Voice to Customer Support
use_cases.description: Plan a voice-enabled chatbot that uses server VAD, transcription, and function calls for support tasks.

use_cases.title: Evaluate Realtime Audio Patterns
use_cases.description: Compare turn detection, audio formats, and event handling before integrating the SDK into a larger system.

prompt_templates.title: Create a Minimal Connection
prompt_templates.prompt: Use azure-ai-voicelive-py to outline a minimal Python connection with DefaultAzureCredential and text plus audio modalities.

prompt_templates.title: Configure Turn Detection
prompt_templates.prompt: Use azure-ai-voicelive-py to compare server VAD and manual turn mode for a Python voice assistant.

prompt_templates.title: Handle Streaming Events
prompt_templates.prompt: Use azure-ai-voicelive-py to design an event handling flow for transcripts, audio deltas, response completion, and errors.

prompt_templates.title: Design Function Calling
prompt_templates.prompt: Use azure-ai-voicelive-py to plan a validated function calling flow for a voice assistant that can call business tools.

output_examples.input: A developer asks for a basic Azure Voice Live application structure.
output_examples.output: A concise plan covering environment variables, authentication, session creation, event listening, and shutdown handling.

output_examples.input: A team needs to choose audio and turn detection settings.
output: Recommended default audio format for quality.
Suggested VAD settings for natural conversation.
Notes about telephony formats and manual turn control.

output_examples.input: An architect asks how function calling should be added safely.
output_examples.output: A workflow that validates tool names and arguments, limits callable actions, logs results, and returns controlled outputs.

best_practices: Use DefaultAzureCredential where possible and avoid hardcoding API keys.
Treat microphone audio and transcripts as sensitive user data.
Validate function call names, arguments, permissions, and outputs before execution.
anti_patterns: Do not log full transcripts or audio data without a clear retention policy.
Do not expose Azure API keys in source files, prompts, or shared chat history.
Do not execute model-requested functions without allowlists and input validation.

faq.question: What does this skill help build?
faq.answer: It helps build Python applications that use Azure AI Voice Live for realtime voice conversations.

faq.question: Does it include a complete application?
faq.answer: No. It provides patterns and examples for the SDK, but application wiring is still required.

faq.question: Which authentication method should I use?
faq.answer: Use DefaultAzureCredential when possible. Use API keys only when your environment requires them.

faq.question: Does the skill process live microphone audio?
faq.answer: It shows patterns for streaming audio. Your application must implement capture, consent, playback, and privacy controls.

faq.question: Can it support function calling?
faq.answer: Yes. The skill shows the event pattern, but production systems need validation and authorization.

faq.question: Is it compatible with Claude, Codex, and Claude Code?
faq.answer: Yes. The marketplace metadata lists Claude, Codex, and Claude Code as supported tools.
